rationale: Exceptional on every axis. The opening image of the chairs is immediately concrete and orienting. The catalog of prior jobs is specific to the point of being visceral—the rigged sanitizer thermometer, the thirty-nine-hour schedule, Dale who paid cash 'if he remembered'—without a single wasted detail. The language is plain and muscular; there's no inflation anywhere. The rhythm of the prose (the triadic lists, the short declarative closer) creates momentum without feeling showy. The piece is incomplete—it cuts off before fully delivering on the prompt's 'changed the writer's idea of what work could be'—but what's on the page is so precisely controlled, so alive with voice and specificity, that it earns top marks for what it accomplishes. The final line ('I never questioned any of this') lands with genuine emotional weight. This reads like published literary nonfiction.
